transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Alex. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, well, I guess I’m not really sure what’s important to talk about. I mean, things are just, you know, the same as always. I don’t really want to get into anything too deep or personal. Just, uh, trying to get through the day, I suppose. Maybe we could talk about something else? Like, I don’t know, the weather or something?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a bit uncertain about diving into deeper topics right now, and that’s completely okay. Sometimes, just getting through the day can feel like a lot. If you’d prefer to keep things light for now, we can certainly do that. What’s been on your mind about the weather, or is there something else you’d like to share that feels more comfortable?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I mean, the weather's been, uh, pretty typical, I guess. It’s just, you know, cloudy one minute and then sunny the next. But I don’t really think about it much. I mean, it’s not like it changes anything for me. I just try to stay inside most of the time. It’s easier that way. I don’t really have to deal with, you know, people and all that. So, uh, yeah, I guess that’s all I have to say about that.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the changing weather doesn’t have a big impact on your day-to-day life, and you prefer to stay inside to avoid dealing with people. That makes sense; sometimes being in our own space feels safer or easier. I’m curious, what do you find yourself doing when you’re inside?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Oh, you know, just the usual stuff. I watch some shows or, uh, scroll through my phone. Sometimes I read a bit, but I don’t really have the energy for anything too intense. It’s just kind of nice to zone out, you know? I mean, it’s not like I have a lot of people checking in on me or anything, so it’s just easier to keep to myself. I don’t really see the point in making an effort to, uh, reach out or anything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like zoning out with shows or scrolling helps you unwind, especially when you’re feeling low on energy. It also seems like you’re feeling a bit disconnected from others, which might make reaching out feel less worthwhile. I wonder, what do you think keeps you from wanting to reach out to people, even if it might feel nice to connect?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I guess it’s just that, um, I don’t really feel like I fit in with most people. I mean, whenever I do try to connect, it usually ends up being awkward or, you know, uncomfortable. Plus, there’s always that worry about what they might think of me, like, if they’ll judge me or ask questions I don’t want to answer. It’s just easier to avoid all that and stay in my own bubble. I don’t know, maybe it’s just how things are for me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you often feel out of place when trying to connect with others, and that worry about judgment makes it tough to reach out. Staying in your own bubble feels like a safer option for you. I can see how that might create a cycle of feeling more isolated. When you think about those awkward or uncomfortable moments, what do you feel in those situations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, it’s just a lot of, like, anxiety, I guess. My heart races, and I feel all tense, like I’m just waiting for something to go wrong. It’s kind of overwhelming, and I just want to, you know, escape the situation. I mean, I don’t want to be the center of attention or anything. It’s just, I don’t know, I feel like I’m under a microscope or something. It’s easier to just avoid those moments altogether.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really tough to experience that kind of anxiety, feeling tense and overwhelmed as if you’re being scrutinized. That pressure can make social situations feel really daunting. It makes sense that avoiding those moments feels like the easier choice. I’m curious, have there been times in the past when you felt more comfortable connecting with others, or has it always been this way for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I think it’s always been kind of like this for me. I mean, there might have been a few times when I felt a little more at ease, but those moments are pretty rare. Most of the time, it’s just this nagging feeling that I don’t belong or that I’ll mess things up somehow. So, I guess I just learned to keep my distance. It’s not like I’m missing out on anything too great, right? I mean, it’s just easier to stick to what I know.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like this feeling of not belonging has been a consistent part of your experience, leading you to keep your distance from others. It’s understandable that you’d want to stick to what feels familiar and safe. I hear you saying that you don’t think you’re missing out on much, but I wonder, deep down, do you ever wish things could be different?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I mean, I guess there’s a part of me that thinks it would be nice to, you know, feel more connected or have some kind of support. But then again, I also think about how complicated that can get. It’s just easier to keep things simple and not have to deal with all the expectations or, like, potential disappointments. So, I don’t know. I try not to dwell on it too much. It’s just how things are, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there’s a conflict within you—part of you longs for connection and support, while another part feels that keeping things simple and avoiding complications is safer. It’s understandable to want to protect yourself from potential disappointments. I appreciate your honesty in sharing this. When you think about that desire for connection, what do you think might be the first step, even a small one, toward exploring that possibility?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, I don’t know. I mean, it’s hard to think about taking any steps when everything feels so overwhelming. Maybe, like, just saying hello to someone or, I don’t know, responding to a message or something? But then I get all anxious just thinking about it. It feels like a big deal, and I’m not sure I’m ready for that. I guess I’d have to really think about it before doing anything. It’s just, uh, easier to stay in my comfort zone, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that even small steps can feel overwhelming, especially when anxiety is involved. Saying hello or responding to a message can feel like a big deal when you’re in that headspace. It’s okay to take your time to think about it and to stay in your comfort zone for now. I wonder, what do you think would help you feel a bit more at ease with the idea of reaching out, even if it’s just a small step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess if I had some kind of, like, support or encouragement, that might help. You know, just someone to remind me it’s okay to take things slow and that I’m not going to be judged or anything. Maybe even just talking it through with someone before I actually do anything could make it feel less daunting. But, uh, I don’t know if I’d actually go through with it. It’s just a lot to think about, and I’m not sure I’m ready to put myself out there yet.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having support and encouragement could make a difference for you, helping to ease that feeling of being judged and making the process feel less daunting. Talking things through before taking any steps feels like a valuable way to prepare. It’s completely okay to feel unsure about putting yourself out there right now. Taking your time and exploring these feelings is important. If you ever want to talk more about what that support might look like or how to navigate those feelings, I’m here for that. What do you think would be most helpful for you in this moment?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, I think just having someone listen, like you’re doing now, is helpful. It’s nice to be able to talk without feeling pressured to, you know, come up with solutions or anything right away. I guess just knowing that I can take my time and that it’s okay to feel how I feel is a bit comforting. I don’t really have to rush into anything, and that’s, um, kind of a relief. So, yeah, just talking it out is good for now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that talking it out feels comforting for you. It’s important to have space to express your thoughts and feelings without pressure. You’re right; there’s no rush, and it’s okay to take your time. If there’s anything else on your mind or anything specific you’d like to explore further, I’m here to listen. What else would you like to share today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I guess I’ve been thinking a bit about, um, how I often feel like I’m just going through the motions, you know? Like, I wake up, do what I have to do, and then it’s just rinse and repeat. It’s not like I’m really enjoying anything or feeling fulfilled. I mean, I don’t know if that’s normal or if everyone feels like that sometimes. It’s just, uh, kind of a heavy feeling, I guess. But I’m not sure what to do about it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a sense of monotony in your daily routine, where it feels like you’re just going through the motions without much enjoyment or fulfillment. That heaviness can be really challenging to carry. It’s not uncommon for people to feel this way at times, but it’s important to acknowledge how it affects you. I’m curious, when you think about those moments when you feel like you’re just going through the motions, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I think it’s mostly just, like, a sense of emptiness, maybe? Sometimes I feel a bit sad or, I don’t know, frustrated that things aren’t different. It’s like I want to feel something more, but I just can’t seem to get there. I guess it’s a little lonely, too, since I don’t really have anyone to share those feelings with. So, I just keep it all bottled up, which probably doesn’t help. It’s just, uh, hard to break out of that cycle, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that sense of emptiness brings up feelings of sadness and frustration, along with a sense of loneliness from not having someone to share those feelings with. Bottling things up can definitely make it harder to break out of that cycle. I can see how that would feel heavy and isolating. When you think about wanting to feel something more, what does that look like for you? What do you imagine would bring you a sense of fulfillment or connection?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, I don’t know, really. I guess I imagine doing things that, like, spark some joy or excitement, but I’m not sure what that would be. Maybe spending time with people who actually get me or, I don’t know, finding a hobby that I really enjoy. But then I think about how hard it is to even start something new, and it just feels overwhelming. I mean, it’s like I want to feel connected, but I also worry about how to make that happen. It’s just a lot to think about, and I’m not sure where to begin.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have a desire for joy and excitement, whether through connecting with people who understand you or exploring new hobbies. Yet, the thought of starting something new feels overwhelming, which is completely understandable. It’s a lot to navigate. I wonder, if you could imagine just one small step toward exploring something that might spark joy or connection, what might that look like for you? It could be something very simple.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess maybe just, like, looking up a local group or class for something I might be interested in? I mean, it doesn’t have to be anything big, just something to see what’s out there. But then again, I’d probably feel anxious about actually going or meeting new people. So, I don’t know if I’d really follow through with it. It’s just a thought, but it feels like a lot to take on. I guess I’d have to really work up the courage to even consider it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Looking up a local group or class sounds like a thoughtful first step, even if it feels a bit daunting. It’s completely normal to feel anxious about the idea of meeting new people, especially when you’re considering stepping out of your comfort zone. It’s okay to take your time with this and to acknowledge that it might take some courage to follow through. What if you allowed yourself to just explore the idea of those groups or classes without any pressure to commit right away? How does that feel to you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, that actually sounds a bit more manageable, I guess. Just exploring the idea without any pressure to, like, actually go or commit right away feels less overwhelming. I could just look up some options and see what’s out there, maybe even just for fun. It’s like taking a little peek without jumping in headfirst, you know? I think I could handle that. It’s still a bit scary, but maybe not as much if I don’t have to think about actually doing it right away.
